Spinothalamic tract:
- pain & thermal sensations
- course touch and pressure
1st order neurone:
from skin to dorsal spinal cord through spinal nerve
or to brainstem through trigeminal nerve
synapses: nucleus propius
cell bodies: dorsal root ganglion

2nd order neurone:
the axon decussates to the opposite cord by passing through ventral white commissure and carry to brainstem. then, terminate in the ventral posterior nucleus of the thalamus.
synapses: ventral posterior nucleus

3rd order neurone:
it projects to the somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus)
cell bodies: ventral posterior nucleus
synapses: somatosensory cortex

dorsal column:
- carry info of proprioception
- fine touch
- located between the dorsal median sulcus and dorsal horn
- comprised of two tracts:
1. fasciculus gracilis
- sacral, lumbar and lower thoracic
cell bodies: nucleus gracilis
synapses: gracile nucleus

2. fasicuclus cuneatus
- upper thoracic, cervical dorsal roots and upper limb
cell bodies: nucleus cuneatus
synapses: cuneate nucleus

2nd neurone:
they decussate in the medulla as internal arcuate fibres and then ascend through the brainstem as the medial lemniscus.
synapses: ventral posterior nucleus

3rd neurone: somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus)

alpha1 adrenoceptor:
NA binds to Gq protein. the target protein is phospholipase C. it splits apart into sub-units which are DAG and IP3. IP3 binds to endoplasmic reticulum and causes the release of Ca2+. then, the smooth muscle contracts.
DAG generates effect itself.

alpha2 adrenoceptor:
NA binds to Gi protein. the target protein is adenylyl cyclase. it decreases the level of cAMP, activity of protein kinase A and phosphorylation of intracellular proteins. This causes the inhibition of neurotransmitter.

beta adrenoceptor:
NA binds to Gs protein. the target protein is adenylyl cyclase. it increases the level of cAMP, activity of protein kinase A and phosphorylation of intracellular proteins. this causes the relaxation of smooth muscle.

agonist:
a1: phenylephrine
a2: clonidine
b1: dobutamine
b2: salbutamol

antagonist:
a1: prozasin
a2: yohimbine
b1: alenolol
b2: butoxamine

botulinum:
1st part: it binds to postsynaptic membrane
2nd part: it cleaves the proteins involved in exocytosis and inhibits exocytosis. the prolonged depolarisation and blockage of cholinergic receptors cause muscle paralysis.

treatment:
use 4-aminopyridine. it blocks K+ channels which reduces the efflux of K+. this causes the opening of Ca+ channel. the influx of Ca+ channel can cause exocytosis.

ibuprofen:
prostaglandin is the mediator of pain, fever and inflammatory.
the nsaid blocks COX which is an essential enzyme for the synthesis of prostaglandin. it enters the hydrophobic channel and forms H-bond with arginine residue. it prevents substrate fatty acid from entering catalytic domain.

it associates with an increased risk of gastric ulcers because its acidic molecules irritate the gastric mucosa directly and it inhibits the synthesis of prostaglandin while prostaglandin helps reducing gastric acid output. ibuprofen increases the secretion of gastic acid.
GA:
inhalation: isoflurane
it inibits the action of GABAa receptors.
intravenous: propofol
it inhibits the action of GABAa receptors
Ketamine:
it is the competitive antagonist of NMDA receptors.
